title: When an effect is needed

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for When an effect is needed; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to When an effect is needed
sources.claim: An effect synchronizes React state with an external system after commit; derived render data and event consequences usually need no effect.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An effect synchronizes React state with an external system after commit; derived render data and event consequences usually need no effect.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A media-player effect calls play or pause to synchronize an external video element with isPlaying. Your goal is to remove an unnecessary effect and place logic in render or an event handler. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: State and events; javascript async network; Minimal and normalized state; Render purity and Strict Mode. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: An effect synchronizes React state with an external system after commit; derived render data and event consequences usually need no effect. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Effects do not run on the server, can repeat in development, and set-state loops indicate derived-state misuse. This draft therefore rejects: Any calculation after state changes belongs in useEffect.

includes: Use effects only to synchronize with external systems, not for event logic or derivable values.
Remove an unnecessary effect and place logic in render or an event handler.
excludes: Effects do not run on the server, can repeat in development, and set-state loops indicate derived-state misuse.

outcomes: Remove an unnecessary effect and place logic in render or an event handler.

examples.title: When an effect is needed: concrete case
examples.situation: A media-player effect calls play or pause to synchronize an external video element with isPlaying.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A media-player effect calls play or pause to synchronize an external video element with isPlaying.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Calculate render-derived values during render; use effects only to synchronize with systems outside React.
examples.result: The result follows because Calculate render-derived values during render; use effects only to synchronize with systems outside React.

examples.title: When an effect is needed: boundary case
examples.situation: Effects do not run on the server, can repeat in development, and set-state loops indicate derived-state misuse.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Effects do not run on the server, can repeat in development, and set-state loops indicate derived-state misuse.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Any calculation after state changes belongs in useEffect.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Calculate render-derived values during render; use effects only to synchronize with systems outside React.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any calculation after state changes belongs in useEffect.
misconceptions.correction: Calculate render-derived values during render; use effects only to synchronize with systems outside React.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Any calculation after state changes belongs in useEffect.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model When an effect is needed, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for When an effect is needed before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to remove an unnecessary effect and place logic in render or an event handler. Keep this boundary in view: Effects do not run on the server, can repeat in development, and set-state loops indicate derived-state misuse.
